content.problemStatement:
There are three cities and thus three EVMs. An insider told Chef that his party got A,B,CA, B, CA,B,C votes respectively in these three cities according to the EVMs. Also, the total number of votes cast are P,Q,RP, Q, RP,Q,R respectively for the three cities.

Chef, being the party leader, can hack **at most** one EVM so that his party wins. On hacking a particular EVM all the votes cast in that EVM are counted in favor of Chef's party.

A party must secure **strictly more than half** of the total number of votes cast in order to be considered the winner. Can Chef achieve his objective of winning by hacking at most one EVM?


Input Format

The first line of input contains an integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
Each test case consists of a single line of input, containing six space-separated integers — in order, A,B,C,P,Q,RA, B, C, P, Q, RA,B,C,P,Q,R.


Output Format
For each test case, output in a single line the answer — `"YES"`, if Chef can win the election after hacking **at most** one EVM and `"NO"` if not.


You may print each character of the string in uppercase or lowercase (for example, the strings "yEs", "yes", "Yes" and "YES" will all be treated as identical).
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains six space‑separated integers A B C P Q R describing one test case.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "YES" if Chef can become the winner by hacking at most one EVM, otherwise output "NO".

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: total=9, half=4.5. Original votes=3 (not enough). Hacking any city gives 5 votes >4.5 → YES.\nTest case 2: total=200, half=100. Original votes=99. Hacking the second city gives 49+100+49=198 >100 → YES.\nTest case 3: total=3, half=1.5. Even after hacking any city Chef gets only 1 vote ≤1.5 → NO.

solution.approach: For each test case compute total = P+Q+R and half = total/2. Chef wins if his votes are strictly greater than half. His current votes are cur = A+B+C. If 2*cur > total, answer is YES. Otherwise try hacking each city: after hacking city i, Chef's votes become (total of that city) + votes of the other two cities. That is, hack1 = P + B + C, hack2 = A + Q + C, hack3 = A + B + R. If any of 2*hackX > total, answer is YES, else NO. The check 2*votes > total avoids floating point.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work per test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few integer variables.

code.Python3:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out = []
    for _ in range(T):
        A = int(next(it)); B = int(next(it)); C = int(next(it))
        P = int(next(it)); Q = int(next(it)); R = int(next(it))
        total = P + Q + R
        cur = A + B + C
        ok = (2*cur > total)
        if not ok:
            h1 = P + B + C
            h2 = A + Q + C
            h3 = A + B + R
            ok = (2*h1 > total) or (2*h2 > total) or (2*h3 > total)
        out.append('YES' if ok else 'NO')
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
